critique.summary: The article explains how to install or replace a Linux operating system by downloading a disk image directly to a disk device using commands like curl or wget piped into /dev/sdX. It explores various methods to achieve this, including using rescue images and discusses the challenges of overwriting a running OS disk.
mainPoints: You can install a new OS by downloading a disk image directly to a disk device file such as /dev/sda using curl or wget.
This method leverages the Unix concept that disks are accessible as files, allowing direct writes to the disk device.
Common tools like dd can be used to write disk images efficiently, but direct redirection (e.g., curl > /dev/sda) also works.
Overwriting a running OS disk is problematic because the disk is mounted and in use, often causing crashes.
A practical solution is to boot into a rescue image or another OS where the target disk is unmounted before writing the new image.
The author automated parts of this process using NixOS and discusses potential further automation and alternative protocols for disk image transfer.
critique.truthfulness: The technical explanations and commands described in the article are accurate and consistent with standard Linux and Unix practices. Writing directly to disk device files like /dev/sda is a known method for installing disk images. The challenges of overwriting a mounted root filesystem and the use of rescue images are also well documented in Linux system administration. The article's claims about EFI firmware automatically discovering new EFI system partitions are generally true for many modern systems. However, the article is informal and anecdotal, lacking citations or references to official documentation or multiple perspectives, which would strengthen its reliability.
considerations: Directly writing to /dev/sda without verifying the disk identifier can lead to data loss or system damage.
The article does not emphasize the importance of verifying disk image integrity (e.g., using sha256sum) before flashing.
Security risks exist when piping downloads directly to disk without validating the source or image authenticity.
The method is risky for inexperienced users and should be done with caution, preferably with backups.
The article does not discuss differences in behavior across various hardware or firmware implementations.
Potential issues with partition tables, bootloaders, and EFI configurations after flashing are not deeply explored.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the technical challenges and methods of overwriting a running Linux system's disk, including netbooting, using initramfs, and kernel-level considerations to avoid filesystem corruption. Participants share practical experiences, potential pitfalls, and alternative approaches such as using qemu overlays or migrating the OS into RAM.

comments.author: matja
comments.text:
> How do you unmount your OS’s disk while keeping the OS running to be able to overwrite itself?

I went down a similar rabbit-hole myself, with the goal of safely replacing the Linux installation on a disk that a machine is already running from (e.g. replace a VPS's setup image with one of your own) without needing a KVM-style remote access tool to the console.

The problem there is if you directly modify the disk when a filesystem is mounted on that disk then all bets are off in terms of corruption of the filesystem that's already on there and also the filesystem(s) you're writing over the top.

My solution was to kexec into a new kernel+initramfs which has a DHCP client and cURL in it - that effectively stops any filesystem access while the image is being written over the disk, then to just reboot.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies the risk of corruption when modifying a mounted filesystem and suggests a practical, safe approach using kexec and a minimal environment.

comments.author: rwmj
comments.text:
Unfortunately it's not safe as the kernel can still write to (what it thinks is) the old filesystem on the device, which will introduce corruption to the new disk image.

However a fun fact is that you can (do not actually do this!) boot a qemu VM from /dev/sda. You have to use an overlay (eg. qemu -drive snapshot=on flag) so that qemu won't write through to /dev/sda. I use this trick in supernested, a script I wrote that runs nested within nested within nested VMs ad infinitum until your hypervisor crashes. http://git.annexia.org/?p=supernested.git;a=blob;f=run-super...
comments.analysis: Provides a technically accurate warning about corruption risks and shares an advanced workaround involving qemu overlays, demonstrating deep technical knowledge.

comments.author: alexellisuk
comments.text:
This reminds me of netbooting workflows from things like MaaS, Tinkerbell, and Dan's old Plunder tool.

They'd netboot.. not mount the disks, then download an ISO/IMG and write it directly to the primary boot disk.

If netbooting is a heavy lift, why not boot into a custom initramfs you built, with i.e. dd/curl installed, and flash the disk that way, without mounting / at all? Then kexec/chroot into it?

I'd much prefer this as a way to provision Raspberry Pis.
comments.analysis: Offers a relevant and practical alternative approach to provisioning devices safely without mounting the disk, aligning well with best practices.

comments.author: M95D
comments.text:
From the article:

> The OS may stop you from unmounting /dev/sda1, but it won’t stop you from writing to /dev/sda1 or /dev/sda even if there’s something mounted!

Not always true. There's a kernel config option that allows it. CONFIG_BLK_DEV_WRITE_MOUNTED
comments.analysis: Correctly points out a kernel configuration option that affects write permissions on mounted devices, adding nuance to the article's claim.

comments.author: pzmarzly
comments.text:
You will run into problems if destination drive has different sector size than your VM, as GPT header won't be aligned.

QEMU defaults to 512B sectors, which isn't true for many NVMe drives. There are some flags to change that. https://unix.stackexchange.com/a/722450

I think it should be possible to make an image with many headers at different locations, so that it works on all types of disks at once, but I don't think any tools do it for you by default.
comments.analysis: Highlights an important technical detail about sector size mismatches that can cause issues, providing useful information for those using VM images.

comments.author: PunchyHamster
comments.text:
> Well, what can we try instead? > write to the mounted disk anyways. fuck you

Stupid penguin trick I learned: Add a file inside ramdisk (i use /dev/shm) as LVM PV.

pvmove off the hard drive

Boom, now your OS lives entirely in RAM

You can now even replace the hard disk, put a new one and migrate back.

Or migrate to network storage (nbd,iSCSI etc.), re-sequence disks into whatever RAID you need, and migrate back

Need to fix /boot after that tho, and probably make sure to not have power failure in meantime
comments.analysis: Shares an innovative method to migrate the OS into RAM to enable disk replacement, which is a clever and practical approach though somewhat advanced.

comments.author: dizhn
comments.text:
Reminded me of how to install Alpine linux (which isn't available) on Oracle cloud over an ubuntu install. It uses dd and has the advantage of having a console.

I had found it in a github gist when I used it but here's a similar blog post.

https://alextsang.net/articles/20191006-063049/index.html
comments.analysis: Provides a useful real-world example and resource related to the topic, adding practical context.

comments.author: irishcoffee
comments.text:
I've been dd-ing A/B partitions for embedded yocto distributions for years and years. read-only-rootfs (/var/log is its own writable partition), dd the "other partition", sed fstab, reboot.

The neat part was the whole process kicked off when you scp'd the rootfs and inotifywait kicked off the whole process.
comments.analysis: Shares a practical and tested workflow for updating embedded systems, contributing valuable experiential insight.

comments.author: ma2kx
comments.analysis: Poses a simple, relevant question that points to a common alternative method, though without elaboration.

comments.author: PunchyHamster
comments.text: and we've gone full circle, back in the day you installed os on diskettes like that!
comments.analysis: A nostalgic comment that adds little technical value to the discussion.
